abstract:
To survive and thrive in complex environments, humans have evolved
sophisticated self-improvement mechanisms through environment exploration,
hierarchical abstraction of experiences into reuseable skills, and
collaborative construction of an ever-growing skill repertoire. Despite recent
advancements, autonomous web agents still lack crucial self-improvement
capabilities, struggling with procedural knowledge abstraction, refining
skills, and skill composition. In this work, we introduce SkillWeaver, a
skill-centric framework enabling agents to self-improve by autonomously
synthesizing reusable skills as APIs. Given a new website, the agent
autonomously discovers skills, executes them for practice, and distills
practice experiences into robust APIs. Iterative exploration continually
expands a library of lightweight, plug-and-play APIs, significantly enhancing
the agent's capabilities. Experiments on WebArena and real-world websites
demonstrate the efficacy of SkillWeaver, achieving relative success rate
improvements of 31.8% and 39.8%, respectively. Additionally, APIs synthesized
by strong agents substantially enhance weaker agents through transferable
skills, yielding improvements of up to 54.3% on WebArena. These results
demonstrate the effectiveness of honing diverse website interactions into APIs,
which can be seamlessly shared among various web agents.

keyInsights: Weaker agents can achieve 40-133% relative improvements by leveraging APIs discovered by stronger agents
Auto-generated APIs perform comparably to human-crafted ones on websites with varying API support levels
Compositional APIs emerge naturally after sufficient exploration iterations
originalProblem: Existing web agents struggle to generalize across diverse websites and tasks due to overfitting to specific structures
Current approaches lack explicit abstraction of reusable procedural knowledge and require extensive training or supervision
results: 39.8% relative improvement in success rate on real-world websites
Stable performance gains across 5 WebArena and 4 live websites
Synthesized APIs demonstrate comparable quality to official documentation
Successful knowledge transfer between different strength agents through API reuse
solution: Three-stage pipeline for autonomous skill discovery: skill proposal, synthesis, and honing
Conversion of successful action trajectories into reusable Python APIs with built-in verification
Curriculum-based exploration starting with simple skills and progressing to compositional abilities
paper_summary.summary: Carnegie Mellon University and partner institutions develop SkillWeaver, a framework enabling web agents to autonomously discover and synthesize reusable API skills through environmental exploration, achieving 25-38% improvement in success rates on WebArena benchmarks and demonstrating effective skill transfer across different websites and agent capabilities.

title: SkillWeaver: Web Agents can Self-Improve by Discovering and Honing  Skills

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown stunning results in complex tasks, yet they often operate in isolation, failing to learn from past experiences. Existing memory-based methods primarily store raw trajectories, which are often redundant and noise-heavy. This prevents agents from extracting high-level, reusable behavioral patterns that are essential for generalization. In this paper, we propose SkillRL, a framework that bridges the gap between raw experience and policy improvement through automatic skill discovery and recursive evolution. Our approach introduces an experience-based distillation mechanism to build a hierarchical skill library SkillBank, an adaptive retrieval strategy for general and task-specific heuristics, and a recursive evolution mechanism that allows the skill library to co-evolve with the agent's policy during reinforcement learning. These innovations significantly reduce the token footprint while enhancing reasoning utility. Experimental results on ALFWorld, WebShop and seven search-augmented tasks demonstrate that SkillRL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines over 15.3% and maintaining robustness as task complexity increases. Code is available at this this https URL.

keyInsights: Abstracting experiences into high-level, hierarchical skills provides superior performance and context efficiency for LLM agents compared to storing or compressing raw interaction trajectories.

A dynamic and recursive skill evolution mechanism, where the skill library co-evolves with the agent's policy, is critical for continuous improvement, adaptability, and effectively addressing emergent failure modes.
Initial Supervised Fine-Tuning on skill-augmented reasoning traces is essential for enabling the base LLM agent to effectively utilize and integrate abstract skills into its decision-making process.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) agents often operate episodically, failing to learn and generalize from past experiences, which limits their scalability and adaptability.
Existing memory-augmented LLM agents predominantly store raw, verbose, and noisy interaction trajectories, leading to "context bloat" and inefficient extraction of high-level behavioral patterns.
Current memory mechanisms typically function as external references without fundamentally adapting the agent's internal policy or evolving to address new failure modes and challenges.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art success rates, including 89.9% on ALFWorld, 72.7% on WebShop, and an average score of 47.1% on search-augmented QA tasks.
Outperformed strong baselines, including GPT-4o by 41.9% and Gemini-2.5-Pro by 29.6% on ALFWorld, demonstrating that intelligent skill learning can compensate for model scale.

Reduced prompt token usage by approximately 10.3% compared to raw memory retrieval, while also exhibiting faster convergence and a dynamic expansion of the skill library from 55 to 100 skills during training.
solution: Employs a teacher model to distill raw interaction trajectories, including both successful episodes and failures, into concise "strategic patterns" and "failure lessons."

Constructs a hierarchical SKILLBANK that organizes distilled knowledge into general skills (universal strategies) and task-specific skills, designed for efficient retrieval and significant token compression.

Utilizes Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for a cold-start initialization, followed by recursive skill evolution during Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to dynamically refine the skill library and agent policy based on ongoing performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and collaborators developed SKILLRL, a framework enabling Large Language Model (LLM) agents to learn and recursively evolve abstract skills from diverse experiences. SKILLRL achieves state-of-the-art performance with a 89.9% success rate on ALFWorld, 72.7% on WebShop, and an average score of 47.1% on search-augmented QA tasks, outperforming larger closed-source models and memory-augmented baselines.

title: SkillRL: Evolving Agents via Recursive Skill-Augmented Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Autonomous agents powered by large language models (LLMs) have the potential to significantly enhance human productivity by reasoning, using tools, and executing complex tasks in diverse environments. However, current approaches to developing such agents remain costly and inefficient, as they typically require manually constructed task datasets and reinforcement learning (RL) pipelines with extensive random exploration. These limitations lead to prohibitively high data-construction costs, low exploration efficiency, and poor sample utilization. To address these challenges, we present AgentEvolver, a self-evolving agent system that leverages the semantic understanding and reasoning capabilities of LLMs to drive autonomous agent learning. AgentEvolver introduces three synergistic mechanisms: (i) self-questioning, which enables curiosity-driven task generation in novel environments, reducing dependence on handcrafted datasets; (ii) self-navigating, which improves exploration efficiency through experience reuse and hybrid policy guidance; and (iii) self-attributing, which enhances sample efficiency by assigning differentiated rewards to trajectory states and actions based on their contribution. By integrating these mechanisms into a unified framework, AgentEvolver enables scalable, cost-effective, and continual improvement of agent capabilities. Preliminary experiments indicate that AgentEvolver achieves more efficient exploration, better sample utilization, and faster adaptation compared to traditional RL-based baselines.

keyInsights: Large Language Models possess sufficient reasoning capabilities to autonomously drive their own learning process, encompassing task generation, exploration guidance, and reward attribution.
High-quality synthetic training data, generated by LLMs with robust curation and a reference-based LLM Judge, can effectively replace or augment human-curated datasets for agent training.
Combining curiosity-driven task generation, experience-guided exploration, and fine-grained credit assignment mechanisms creates a synergistic system for efficient and scalable agent self-evolution.
originalProblem: The high cost and scarcity of manually crafted, diverse task datasets for training LLM-powered agents.
Inefficient exploration strategies in reinforcement learning (RL) for LLM agents, leading to slow convergence in complex, tool-augmented environments.
Low sample utilization in RL, where sparse terminal rewards fail to provide fine-grained credit assignment for individual actions within a trajectory.
results: Achieved a 29.4 percentage point improvement in average task goal completion (avg@8) for the 7B model and 27.8 percentage points for the 14B model on AppWorld and BFCL v3 benchmarks.
Reduced training steps for convergence by 55% on AppWorld and 67% on BFCL v3 compared to baselines, demonstrating significantly enhanced sample efficiency.
Synthetic training data generated by the system proved as effective as, or superior to, human-curated data, facilitating cross-domain generalization.
solution: Self-Questioning: Enables autonomous generation of diverse training tasks and reference solutions by leveraging LLMs for curiosity-guided environment exploration and adaptive task synthesis.
Self-Navigating: Boosts exploration efficiency by distilling and reusing structured natural language 'experiences' from past successful or failed trajectories to guide future rollouts.
Self-Attributing: Improves sample efficiency through LLM-based retrospective analysis to assign fine-grained, step-wise attribution rewards to individual actions within a trajectory.
paper_summary.summary: AgentEvolver presents a self-evolving agent system that leverages large language models for autonomous task generation, efficient exploration, and fine-grained credit assignment. The framework demonstrated an average performance improvement of 29.4 percentage points for its 7B model and reduced training steps for convergence by up to 67% on standard benchmarks.

title: AgentEvolver: Towards Efficient Self-Evolving Agent System

abstract: Contemporary evaluation techniques are inadequate for agentic systems. These approaches either focus exclusively on final outcomes -- ignoring the step-by-step nature of agentic systems, or require excessive manual labour. To address this, we introduce the Agent-as-a-Judge framework, wherein agentic systems are used to evaluate agentic systems. This is an organic extension of the LLM-as-a-Judge framework, incorporating agentic features that enable intermediate feedback for the entire task-solving process. We apply the Agent-as-a-Judge to the task of code generation. To overcome issues with existing benchmarks and provide a proof-of-concept testbed for Agent-as-a-Judge, we present DevAI, a new benchmark of 55 realistic automated AI development tasks. It includes rich manual annotations, like a total of 365 hierarchical user requirements. We benchmark three of the popular agentic systems using Agent-as-a-Judge and find it dramatically outperforms LLM-as-a-Judge and is as reliable as our human evaluation baseline. Altogether, we believe that Agent-as-a-Judge marks a concrete step forward for modern agentic systems -- by providing rich and reliable reward signals necessary for dynamic and scalable self-improvement.

author_info.institution: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Current AI agents demonstrate limited capabilities in tackling complex, real-world AI application development tasks, as evidenced by solving only one full task out of 55 on the new DevAI benchmark.
Human evaluation for complex, multi-step agentic tasks exhibits considerable inter-rater disagreement (10-30%), underscoring the need for more consistent and automated evaluation methods.

The effectiveness of Agent-as-a-Judge relies critically on modules that ensure factual information flow, such as `graph`, `locate`, `read`, `retrieve`, and `ask`, while modules like `planning` and `memory` did not consistently improve performance in this proof-of-concept.
originalProblem: Existing evaluation methods for AI agentic systems are primarily outcome-centric, failing to provide granular, step-by-step feedback necessary for understanding complex behaviors.
Manual human evaluation of AI agents is prohibitively expensive, time-consuming, and prone to individual biases, hindering large-scale and continuous assessment.

Current code generation benchmarks (e.g., HumanEval, SWE-Bench) are often too narrow or susceptible to models overfitting, not reflecting comprehensive, real-world AI application development challenges.
results: Agent-as-a-Judge (AaJ) consistently outperformed LLM-as-a-Judge, achieving over 90% alignment with human consensus on evaluating agent performance, compared to LLM-as-a-Judge's 60-70%.
AaJ reduced evaluation costs by 97.64% and time by 97.72% compared to human expert evaluation, completing tasks in minutes rather than hours.

The DevAI benchmark revealed that current state-of-the-art developer agents, like GPT-Pilot and OpenHands, could only satisfy approximately 29-44% of hierarchical requirements and solved only one full task out of 55, highlighting the benchmark's difficulty.
solution: Introduces Agent-as-a-Judge (AaJ), an agentic framework designed to evaluate other AI agents by equipping the judge with agentic capabilities to interact with environments and provide intermediate feedback.
Develops DevAI, a new benchmark comprising 55 real-world AI application development tasks, each with 365 hierarchical requirements, to provide detailed, non-sparse reward signals.
The AaJ framework employs a modular architecture, with an empirical focus on high-performing components like `graph`, `locate`, `read`, `retrieve`, and `ask` for robust and factual judgment.
paper_summary.summary: The Agent-as-a-Judge framework and the DevAI benchmark provide a scalable and reliable method for evaluating AI agents, demonstrating a superior alignment with human judgment compared to LLM-as-a-Judge. This approach achieves substantial cost and time reductions in evaluating complex, real-world AI application development tasks.

title: Agent-as-a-Judge: Evaluate Agents with Agents

abstract: Most Large Language Model (LLM) agent memory systems rely on a small set of static, hand-designed operations for extracting memory. These fixed procedures hard-code human priors about what to store and how to revise memory, making them rigid under diverse interaction patterns and inefficient on long histories. To this end, we present \textbf{MemSkill}, which reframes these operations as learnable and evolvable memory skills, structured and reusable routines for extracting, consolidating, and pruning information from interaction traces. Inspired by the design philosophy of agent skills, MemSkill employs a \emph{controller} that learns to select a small set of relevant skills, paired with an LLM-based \emph{executor} that produces skill-guided memories. Beyond learning skill selection, MemSkill introduces a \emph{designer} that periodically reviews hard cases where selected skills yield incorrect or incomplete memories, and evolves the skill set by proposing refinements and new skills. Together, MemSkill forms a closed-loop procedure that improves both the skill-selection policy and the skill set itself. Experiments on LoCoMo, LongMemEval, HotpotQA, and ALFWorld demonstrate that MemSkill improves task performance over strong baselines and generalizes well across settings. Further analyses shed light on how skills evolve, offering insights toward more adaptive, self-evolving memory management for LLM agents.

keyInsights: Memory management for LLM agents can be conceptualized as a set of dynamic, reusable memory skills that can be learned and refined over time.
A continuous feedback loop, where an agent learns to utilize skills and a designer periodically evolves these skills based on performance, leads to self-improving memory systems.
Explicitly structuring memory behaviors into an interpretable skill bank promotes adaptability, reusability across tasks, and offers a pathway towards more autonomous LLM agents.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent memory systems rely on static, human-engineered operations, which are rigid and inefficient for long, open-ended interactions.
The hard-coded nature of memory extraction, consolidation, and pruning limits adaptability to diverse interaction patterns and hinders scalability over growing interaction histories.
Prior methods primarily optimize *how to use* a predefined set of memory operations rather than enabling the evolution of the operations themselves.
results: MemSkill consistently achieved superior performance, obtaining the highest LLM-judge scores on conversational benchmarks (LoCoMo, LongMemEval) and the highest success rates on embodied interactive tasks (ALFWorld) compared to strong baselines.

The system demonstrated strong generalization capabilities, with learned skills transferring effectively across different base LLMs (e.g., LLaMA-3 to Qwen3) and diverse datasets (e.g., LoCoMo to HotpotQA) without requiring retraining.

Ablation studies confirmed that both the learned skill selection by the controller and the skill evolution process orchestrated by the designer are critical components contributing to MemSkill's performance gains.
solution: MemSkill reframes memory operations as "learnable and evolvable memory skills" stored in a dynamic "skill bank."
It employs a closed-loop optimization recipe combining reinforcement learning for a controller to select relevant skills and an LLM-guided designer to evolve the skill bank from hard cases.
A fixed LLM executor constructs skill-guided memories for text spans by conditioning on selected skills, generating structured memory updates in a single pass.
paper_summary.summary: MemSkill introduces an approach for Large Language Model (LLM) agents where memory operations are learned and evolve over time, moving beyond static, human-engineered mechanisms. This system, developed by Nanyang Technological University and collaborators, achieves superior performance in conversational and embodied tasks by continually refining a dynamic skill bank and a skill-selection policy.

title: MemSkill: Learning and Evolving Memory Skills for Self-Evolving Agents

abstract: The operational efficacy of large language models relies heavily on their inference-time context. This has established Context Engineering (CE) as a formal discipline for optimizing these inputs. Current CE methods rely on manually crafted harnesses, such as rigid generation-reflection workflows and predefined context schemas. They impose structural biases and restrict context optimization to a narrow, intuition-bound design space. To address this, we introduce Meta Context Engineering (MCE), a bi-level framework that supersedes static CE heuristics by co-evolving CE skills and context artifacts. In MCE iterations, a meta-level agent refines engineering skills via agentic crossover, a deliberative search over the history of skills, their executions, and evaluations. A base-level agent executes these skills, learns from training rollouts, and optimizes context as flexible files and code. We evaluate MCE across five disparate domains under offline and online settings. MCE demonstrates consistent performance gains, achieving 5.6--53.8% relative improvement over state-of-the-art agentic CE methods (mean of 16.9%), while maintaining superior context adaptability, transferability, and efficiency in both context usage and training.

keyInsights: Context Engineering can be formulated as a bi-level optimization problem, allowing for the co-evolution of context generation strategies (skills) and the resulting context artifacts.
"Agentic crossover" provides a flexible and deliberative mechanism for a meta-level agent to synthesize superior context engineering skills by analyzing past performance and task requirements.
Representing context as arbitrary files and code, rather than rigid schemas, enables fully agentic context optimization at the base-level, free from structural biases.
originalProblem: Existing Context Engineering (CE) methods rely on manually crafted "agentic harnesses" that introduce structural biases and rigid context representations.
Current approaches constrain context optimization to an "intuition-bound design space," limiting the discovery of truly task-optimal strategies.
Static CE heuristics result in contexts that lack adaptability, token efficiency, and transferability across various LLM capacities and task complexities.
results: MCE consistently outperformed state-of-the-art context engineering methods, achieving an average relative improvement of 89.1% over base models in offline settings and 74.1% in online settings.
The framework demonstrated superior context efficiency, achieving higher performance with significantly fewer tokens (e.g., 75% accuracy with 20K tokens on FiNER vs. 70% with 79K for ACE).
MCE substantially reduced training duration by 13.6x and required 4.8x fewer rollouts compared to leading baselines, while also enhancing context transferability between LLMs.
solution: MCE introduces a bi-level optimization framework that enables the co-evolution of Context Engineering (CE) "skills" (strategies) and context "artifacts."
A meta-level agent iteratively refines CE skills using "agentic crossover," synthesizing superior strategies by reasoning over historical data.
A base-level agent executes these evolved skills to construct and optimize context, represented as flexible files and code, using coding toolkits and file system access.
paper_summary.summary: Meta Context Engineering (MCE) enables large language model agents to autonomously co-evolve their context engineering strategies (skills) and context artifacts through a bi-level optimization framework. This method enhances performance and efficiency by dynamically adapting context properties, yielding an average relative improvement of 89.1% over base models and outperforming state-of-the-art context engineering methods.

title: Meta Context Engineering via Agentic Skill Evolution

abstract: Coding agents are increasingly used as general-purpose problem solvers, but their flexibility does not by itself confer the domain expertise needed for specialized tasks. Recent work addresses this through \textit{agent skills}: reusable workflows, and code, that augment agents with domain-specific capabilities. Most skills today are hand-crafted, and existing evolutionary approaches optimize low-level artifacts (e.g. prompts \& code) that are tightly coupled to specific models and tasks. We introduce \textbf{EvoSkill}, a self-evolving framework that automatically discovers and refines agent skills through iterative failure analysis. EvoSkill analyzes execution failures, proposes new skills or edits to existing ones, and materializes them into structured, reusable skill folders. A Pareto frontier of agent programs governs selection, retaining only skills that improve held-out validation performance while the underlying model remains frozen. We evaluate EvoSkill on two benchmarks: OfficeQA, a grounded reasoning benchmark over U.S.\ Treasury data, where it improves exact-match accuracy by \textbf{7.3\%} (60.6\% $\to$ 67.9\%); and SealQA, a search-augmented QA benchmark with noisy retrieval, where it yields a \textbf{12.1\%} gain (26.6\% $\to$ 38.7\%). We also investigate the zero-shot transfer capabilties of skills evolved on one task to the other; in particular: skills evolved from SealQA transfers zero-shot to BrowseComp, improving accuracy by \textbf{5.3\%} without modification demonstrating that skill-level optimization produces transferable capabilities beyond the training task.

keyInsights: Iterative, failure-driven textual feedback from a specialized LLM-based Proposer can effectively guide the automated discovery and refinement of agent skills, leading to continuous performance improvements.

Optimizing agent capabilities at the "skill" abstraction level yields more interpretable, composable, and crucially, transferable knowledge compared to optimizing lower-level artifacts like prompts or raw code.
Skills discovered in independent evolutionary runs can be complementary, and their aggregation into a combined library leads to a more comprehensive and robust set of agent capabilities.
originalProblem: Coding agents, despite their general flexibility, often lack specialized domain expertise for consistently high performance on complex tasks.
Manually crafting domain-specific skills for agents is resource-intensive, requires significant human expertise, and does not scale effectively with increasing task complexity.

Existing automated methods for agent improvement, such as optimizing low-level prompts or code, result in artifacts that are tightly coupled to specific models and tasks, limiting reusability and transferability.
results: On the OfficeQA benchmark, EvoSkill consistently improved exact-match accuracy over the baseline, with a skill-merge configuration achieving a substantial +7.3 percentage point gain (from 60.6% to 67.9%).
For the SealQA benchmark, EvoSkill significantly enhanced accuracy by +12.1 percentage points (from 26.6% to 38.7%), demonstrating its effectiveness on challenging search-intensive tasks.

A "search-persistence-protocol" skill, evolved on SealQA, successfully transferred zero-shot to the unseen BrowseComp benchmark, yielding a +5.3 percentage point accuracy improvement (from 43.5% to 48.8%).
solution: EvoSkill proposes a self-evolving framework utilizing three collaborating LLM-based agents: an Executor (runs tasks), a Proposer (diagnoses failures and generates high-level skill proposals), and a Skill-Builder (materializes proposals into concrete, structured skills).

The framework operates within an iterative loop that selects agent programs, collects failures, generates skill proposals based on textual feedback and historical data, builds candidate skills, and updates a Pareto frontier of successful programs.

Skills are optimized at a higher level of abstraction, focusing on discovering and refining structured, reusable skill modules with trigger conditions and procedural instructions, rather than directly modifying low-level prompts or code.
paper_summary.summary: EvoSkill introduces a self-evolving framework that autonomously discovers and refines reusable agent skills by analyzing execution failures and leveraging textual feedback. The system demonstrated consistent performance improvements on challenging benchmarks like OfficeQA (up to +7.3 percentage points exact-match accuracy) and SealQA (+12.1 percentage points accuracy), and showed zero-shot skill transferability to unseen tasks.

title: EvoSkill: Automated Skill Discovery for Multi-Agent Systems

abstract: Equipping Large Language Model (LLM) agents with domain-specific skills is critical for tackling complex tasks. Yet, manual authoring creates a severe scalability bottleneck. Conversely, automated skill generation often yields fragile or fragmented results because it either relies on shallow parametric knowledge or sequentially overfits to non-generalizable trajectory-local lessons. To overcome this, we introduce Trace2Skill, a framework that mirrors how human experts author skills: by holistically analyzing broad execution experience before distilling it into a single, comprehensive guide. Instead of reacting sequentially to individual trajectories, Trace2Skill dispatches a parallel fleet of sub-agents to analyze a diverse pool of executions. It extracts trajectory-specific lessons and hierarchically consolidates them into a unified, conflict-free skill directory via inductive reasoning. Trace2Skill supports both deepening existing human-written skills and creating new ones from scratch. Experiments in challenging domains, such as spreadsheet, VisionQA and math reasoning, show that Trace2Skill significantly improves upon strong baselines, including Anthropic's official xlsx skills. Crucially, this trajectory-grounded evolution does not merely memorize task instances or model-specific quirks: evolved skills transfer across LLM scales and generalize to OOD settings. For example, skills evolved by Qwen3.5-35B on its own trajectories improved a Qwen3.5-122B agent by up to 57.65 absolute percentage points on WikiTableQuestions. Ultimately, our results demonstrate that complex agent experience can be packaged into highly transferable, declarative skills -- requiring no parameter updates, no external retrieval modules, and utilizing open-source models as small as 35B parameters.

keyInsights: Agent experience can be distilled into transferable, declarative skills that generalize across LLM scales and out-of-distribution tasks, challenging the assumption that experience is inherently model- and task-specific.
Parallel, holistic consolidation of execution trajectories significantly outperforms sequential online editing and retrieval-based memory systems in both skill quality and computational efficiency.

Robust skill evolution, including both deepening existing human skills and creating new ones from scratch, is achievable using smaller, open-source LLMs (e.g., 35B parameters), reducing dependency on proprietary models.
originalProblem: Manually authoring and maintaining high-quality, domain-specific skills for LLM agents is resource-intensive and does not scale well.
Existing automated skill generation methods often lead to fragmented skill collections, premature overfitting to local lessons, and difficulties with retrieval-based memory systems.
Current automated approaches for skill evolution differ from how human experts consolidate broad understanding into comprehensive, generalized skill documents.
results: Skills evolved by a Qwen3.5-35B model improved a Qwen3.5-122B agent by up to 57.65 absolute percentage points on WikiTableQuestions, demonstrating strong cross-model and out-of-distribution transferability.

Trace2Skill reliably strengthened human-written skills (e.g., +21.5 pp on SpreadsheetBench-Verified) and substantially outperformed parametric baselines for skill creation, often matching or exceeding human-written quality.

Parallel consolidation was approximately 20 times faster than sequential updates and outperformed a ReasoningBank-style retrieval baseline (e.g., +13.8 pp on SpreadsheetBench-Verified) by inductive compression and active deduplication.
solution: Trace2Skill employs a three-stage pipeline (Trajectory Generation, Parallel Multi-Agent Patch Proposal, Conflict-Free Patch Consolidation) to process diverse agent execution experiences.
A fleet of specialized sub-agents (Success Analysts and Error Analysts) independently proposes skill patches from successful and failing trajectories in parallel.

A hierarchical merging process inductively consolidates proposed patches into a single, comprehensive, conflict-free skill document, prioritizing generalizable patterns and resolving conflicts programmatically.
paper_summary.summary: The Trace2Skill framework enables the distillation of LLM agent execution trajectories into transferable, declarative skills, converting diverse agent experiences into comprehensive guides. This approach leads to performance improvements of up to 57.65 absolute percentage points on tasks like WikiTableQuestions and demonstrates effective generalization across different model scales and out-of-distribution task domains.

title: Trace2Skill: Distill Trajectory-Local Lessons into Transferable Agent Skills

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in complex reasoning and multi-turn interactions but struggle to continuously improve and adapt when deployed in new environments. One promising approach is implementing skill libraries that allow agents to learn, validate, and apply new skills. However, current skill library approaches rely primarily on LLM prompting, making consistent skill library implementation challenging. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based approach to enhance agents' self-improvement capabilities with a skill library. Specifically, we introduce Skill Augmented GRPO for self-Evolution (SAGE), a novel RL framework that systematically incorporates skills into learning. The framework's key component, Sequential Rollout, iteratively deploys agents across a chain of similar tasks for each rollout. As agents navigate through the task chain, skills generated from previous tasks accumulate in the library and become available for subsequent tasks. Additionally, the framework enhances skill generation and utilization through a Skill-integrated Reward that complements the original outcome-based rewards. Experimental results on AppWorld demonstrate that SAGE, when applied to supervised-finetuned model with expert experience, achieves 8.9% higher Scenario Goal Completion while requiring 26% fewer interaction steps and generating 59% fewer tokens, substantially outperforming existing approaches in both accuracy and efficiency.

keyInsights: Explicitly rewarding skill generation and usage within an RL framework (Skill-integrated Reward) significantly enhances the adoption and effectiveness of learned skills in LLM agents.
Training agents on sequential tasks (Sequential Rollout) enables the validation of skill quality over multiple interactions, providing better feedback for skill learning.

Supervised fine-tuning with high-quality expert demonstrations is crucial for initializing LLM agents to effectively follow the skill library agent format and achieve optimal performance with subsequent RL training.
originalProblem: LLM-based agents struggle with continuous improvement and adaptation to new environments after deployment, limiting their effectiveness in dynamic scenarios.
Existing skill library implementations rely on manual LLM prompting, resulting in inconsistent skill quality and limited adaptability tied to the base model's instruction-following capabilities.
Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) approaches typically focus on individual task outcomes, lacking explicit mechanisms to foster the development and effective utilization of reusable skills.
results: SAGE achieved 72.0% Task Goal Completion (TGC) and 60.7% Scenario Goal Completion (SGC) on the AppWorld Test Normal dataset, representing an 8.9% increase in SGC over the baseline GRPO method.
The framework improved efficiency by requiring 26% fewer average interaction steps (12.1 vs. 16.4) and generating 59% fewer tokens (1,475 vs. 3,613) compared to the baseline GRPO.

SAGE-trained open-source models (Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct) surpassed the performance of the proprietary expert model (Claude Sonnet 3.5 V2 ReAct) that was used to generate the supervised fine-tuning data.
solution: Introduced Skill Augmented GRPO for self-Evolution (SAGE), a novel reinforcement learning framework that systematically incorporates skill learning into LLM agent training.

Developed a specialized skill library agent that unifies task solving and skill generation, allowing agents to generate, use, update, and save skill functions (programmatic actions) during task execution.

Extended the GRPO algorithm with 'Sequential Rollout' (training on chains of similar tasks to allow skill feedback across tasks) and 'Skill-integrated Reward' (providing explicit bonuses for successful skill generation and utilization).
paper_summary.summary: The SAGE reinforcement learning framework, developed by a team from the University of Wisconsin–Madison and AWS Agentic AI, systematically integrates skill learning into LLM agent training. This framework enhances agent self-improvement by enabling dynamic skill generation and utilization, leading to an 8.9% higher scenario goal completion, 26% fewer interaction steps, and 59% fewer generated tokens on the AppWorld dataset compared to baseline methods.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Self-Improving Agent with Skill Library

abstract: Repurposing large vision-language models (LVLMs) as computer use agents (CUAs) has led to substantial breakthroughs, primarily driven by human-labeled data. However, these models often struggle with novel and specialized software, particularly in scenarios lacking human annotations. To address this challenge, we propose SEAgent, an agentic self-evolving framework enabling CUAs to autonomously evolve through interactions with unfamiliar software. Specifically, SEAgent empowers computer-use agents to autonomously master novel software environments via experiential learning, where agents explore new software, learn through iterative trial-and-error, and progressively tackle auto-generated tasks organized from simple to complex. To achieve this goal, we design a World State Model for step-wise trajectory assessment, along with a Curriculum Generator that generates increasingly diverse and challenging tasks. The agent's policy is updated through experiential learning, comprised of adversarial imitation of failure actions and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) on successful ones. Furthermore, we introduce a specialist-to-generalist training strategy that integrates individual experiential insights from specialist agents, facilitating the development of a stronger generalist CUA capable of continuous autonomous evolution. This unified agent ultimately achieves performance surpassing ensembles of individual specialist agents on their specialized software. We validate the effectiveness of SEAgent across five novel software environments within OS-World. Our approach achieves a significant improvement of 23.2% in success rate, from 11.3% to 34.5%, over a competitive open-source CUA, i.e., UI-TARS.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: An LVLM-based World State Model, when fine-tuned on full trajectory context, can generate precise, step-level reward signals, which are crucial for effective reinforcement learning in complex interactive tasks.

Autonomous curriculum generation, driven by the WSM's feedback, allows agents to progressively learn and master increasingly complex tasks in unfamiliar software environments without human intervention.

A multi-stage specialist-to-generalist training strategy can effectively create a generalist agent that not only generalizes across diverse software but also outperforms the collective performance of individual specialists.
originalProblem: Current Computer Use Agents (CUAs) heavily rely on costly, human-curated datasets, limiting their adaptability to new or specialized software.
Existing reinforcement learning (RL) approaches for agents often struggle with sparse and delayed reward signals across multi-step interactions.
Directly training a single generalist agent for computer use typically leads to suboptimal performance compared to specialized agents.
results: The fine-tuned World State Model achieved state-of-the-art trajectory judgment, significantly narrowing the gap with commercial models like GPT-4o and improving precision by 7.5% over a baseline on AgentRewardBench.
SEAgent demonstrated substantial overall performance, increasing the success rate on OS-World from a baseline of 11.3% to 34.5%, representing a 23.2% absolute gain.

The specialist-to-generalist training strategy achieved the highest overall success rate of 34.5%, outperforming both an ensemble of individual specialists (32.2%) and direct generalist training (30.6%).

Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of the World State Model as the reward source and the superiority of Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) combined with adversarial imitation for improved performance.
solution: SEAgent proposes an autonomous exploration framework with a self-evolving curriculum, enabling CUAs to learn from experience and continuously generate tasks without human oversight.
A novel World State Model (WSM), based on a fine-tuned LVLM, processes full-trajectory context to provide high-quality, step-level reward signals for reinforcement learning.

A specialist-to-generalist training strategy first trains individual CUA specialists, then distills their knowledge into a generalist model, which is further refined via RL across multiple software environments.
paper_summary.summary: SEAgent introduces a self-evolving framework for Computer Use Agents (CUAs) to autonomously learn and adapt to unfamiliar software environments without human supervision. This framework leverages a novel World State Model for precise step-level rewards and a specialist-to-generalist training strategy, achieving a 34.5% success rate on OS-World benchmarks, a 23.2% absolute gain over a DPO-based baseline.

title: SEAgent: Self-Evolving Computer Use Agent with Autonomous Learning from Experience

abstract: The rapid proliferation of Claude agent skills has raised the central question of how to effectively leverage, manage, and scale the agent skill ecosystem. In this paper, we propose AgentSkillOS, the first principled framework for skill selection, orchestration, and ecosystem-level management. AgentSkillOS comprises two stages: (i) Manage Skills, which organizes skills into a capability tree via node-level recursive categorization for efficient discovery; and (ii) Solve Tasks, which retrieves, orchestrates, and executes multiple skills through DAG-based pipelines. To evaluate the agent's ability to invoke skills, we construct a benchmark of 30 artifact-rich tasks across five categories: data computation, document creation, motion video, visual design, and web interaction. We assess the quality of task outputs using LLM-based pairwise evaluation, and the results are aggregated via a Bradley-Terry model to produce unified quality scores. Experiments across three skill ecosystem scales (200 to 200K skills) show that tree-based retrieval effectively approximates oracle skill selection, and that DAG-based orchestration substantially outperforms native flat invocation even when given the identical skill this http URL findings confirm that structured composition is the key to unlocking skill potential. Our GitHub repository is available at:this https URL.

keyInsights: Structured skill management via a capability tree effectively overcomes the scalability bottleneck inherent in vast, unstructured LLM agent skill ecosystems.
DAG-based orchestration of skills is crucial for enabling agents to tackle complex, multi-step tasks, significantly outperforming flat, unstructured skill invocation.
The proposed framework empowers LLM agents to generate high-quality, professional, and multi-modal artifacts, demonstrating capabilities beyond simple text-based outputs.
originalProblem: The rapid proliferation of decentralized LLM agent skills creates overwhelming challenges in discovery, understanding, and management for users and platforms.
Existing tool-augmented and multi-skill agent research struggles to scale to ecosystem-level problems, where hundreds of thousands of skills exist with varying quality and overlaps.
Without explicit mechanisms for composition and coordination, the potential for orchestrating multiple skills to solve complex, multi-step tasks remains largely untapped.
results: AgentSkillOS variants consistently achieved the highest Bradley–Terry scores across all ecosystem sizes (200, 1K, 200K skills), substantially outperforming 'w/ Full Pool' and 'Vanilla' baselines.

The tree-based retrieval mechanism effectively approximated oracle skill selection, allowing agents to discover highly relevant skills from vast ecosystems with only modest performance differences compared to using ground-truth skills.
The framework produced substantially more professional and usable multi-modal artifacts, such as images, videos, and complex documents, compared to vanilla Claude Code baselines.
solution: AgentSkillOS constructs an offline 'capability tree' using LLMs for recursive categorization and skill assignment, facilitating efficient skill retrieval and ecosystem management.

An agent component utilizes the capability tree for task-driven skill retrieval and orchestrates selected skills into a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) for execution, following quality-first, efficiency-first, or simplicity-first strategies.

A specialized benchmark of 30 artifact-rich tasks across five categories was designed, employing pairwise comparative evaluation with an LLM judge and the Bradley-Terry model for robust performance assessment.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Shanghai AI Lab developed AgentSkillOS, a computational framework designed to manage and orchestrate large, decentralized ecosystems of LLM agent skills through a structured capability tree and DAG-based task execution. The system consistently achieved higher performance scores and produced superior quality, multi-modal artifacts for complex tasks compared to baseline approaches.

title: Organizing, Orchestrating, and Benchmarking Agent Skills at Ecosystem Scale

abstract: Current Large Language Model (LLM) agents show strong performance in tool use, but lack the crucial capability to systematically learn from their own experiences. While existing frameworks mainly focus on mitigating external knowledge gaps, they fail to address a more fundamental limitation: the inability to iteratively refine problem-solving strategies. In this work, we introduce EvolveR, a framework designed to enable agent to self-improve through a complete, closed-loop experience lifecycle. This lifecycle comprises two key stages: (1) Offline Self-Distillation, where the agent's interaction trajectories are synthesized into a structured repository of abstract, reusable strategic principles; (2) Online Interaction, where the agent interacts with tasks and actively retrieves distilled principles to guide its decision-making, accumulating a diverse set of behavioral trajectories. This loop employs a policy reinforcement mechanism to iteratively update the agent based on its performance. We demonstrate the effectiveness of EvolveR on complex multi-hop question-answering benchmarks, where it achieves superior performance over strong agentic baselines. Our work presents a comprehensive blueprint for agents that learn not only from external data but also from the consequences of their own actions, paving the way for more autonomous and continuously improving systems. Code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Principles self-distilled by the agent's own policy model are more effective for larger models due to better "cognitive alignment" than those provided by external teacher models.

Experience retrieval from the dynamically curated experience base is an indispensable component, confirming that agents learn not just to generate good trajectories, but how to effectively apply their accumulated wisdom.

Direct internalization of principles into model parameters without a dynamic quality filter for retrieved principles can degrade performance, emphasizing the importance of the explicit, curated experience base.
originalProblem: LLM agents suffer from "operational amnesia," failing to systematically learn from their own experiences and iteratively refine problem-solving strategies.
Existing approaches either discard experiences, store raw un-distilled trajectories, or rely on external teacher models, rather than allowing agents to autonomously distill principles.
Current LLM agents lack a self-contained lifecycle for continuous self-improvement, treating each task as an isolated episode.
results: EVOLVER achieved the highest average Exact Match (EM) score of 0.382 across seven diverse question-answering benchmarks using the Qwen2.5-3B model.
The framework consistently outperformed a range of strong baselines, including advanced prompting, SFT, and state-of-the-art RL agents, demonstrating robust generalizability.

Performance scaled positively with model size, improving monotonically from 0.150 for the 0.5B model to 0.382 for the 3B model, and self-distillation by the 3B model outperformed external teacher distillation (0.382 vs. 0.370 avg. EM).
solution: EVOLVER introduces a closed-loop experience lifecycle comprising Offline Experience Self-Distillation, Online Interaction, and Policy Evolution.

Agents self-distill raw interaction trajectories into abstract strategic principles (guiding or cautionary) using their own policy model, storing them in a dynamic, quality-controlled experience base.

Policy evolution leverages Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a composite reward function to update the agent's policy, learning to effectively utilize distilled wisdom from its experiences.
paper_summary.summary: EVOLVER enables large language model agents to autonomously learn and improve from their own experiences by distilling raw interaction trajectories into strategic principles. This framework achieves an average Exact Match score of 0.382 across seven complex question-answering benchmarks, surpassing various state-of-the-art baselines.

title: EvolveR: Self-Evolving LLM Agents through an Experience-Driven Lifecycle

abstract: Current AI agents can flexibly invoke tools and execute complex tasks, yet their long-term advancement is hindered by the lack of systematic accumulation and transfer of skills. Without a unified mechanism for skill consolidation, agents frequently ``reinvent the wheel'', rediscovering solutions in isolated contexts without leveraging prior strategies. To overcome this limitation, we introduce SkillNet, an open infrastructure designed to create, evaluate, and organize AI skills at scale. SkillNet structures skills within a unified ontology that supports creating skills from heterogeneous sources, establishing rich relational connections, and performing multi-dimensional evaluation across Safety, Completeness, Executability, Maintainability, and Cost-awareness. Our infrastructure integrates a repository of over 200,000 skills, an interactive platform, and a versatile Python toolkit. Experimental evaluations on ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld demonstrate that SkillNet significantly enhances agent performance, improving average rewards by 40% and reducing execution steps by 30% across multiple backbone models. By formalizing skills as evolving, composable assets, SkillNet provides a robust foundation for agents to move from transient experience to durable mastery.

organization_info.name: Hangzhou Institute for Advanced Study, UCAS

keyInsights: Formalizing skills as structured, evaluable, and composable assets is crucial for enabling AI agents to achieve cumulative, transferable mastery over episodic learning.
A comprehensive, multi-dimensional evaluation framework is essential to ensure the reliability and quality of skills within large-scale agent repositories, mitigating "skill pollution."

Organizing skills within an ontology that models rich inter-skill relationships (e.g., 'compose_with', 'depend_on') facilitates global reasoning, efficient retrieval, and robust planning for complex agent tasks.
originalProblem: AI agents lack a systematic mechanism to accumulate and transfer skills from episodic experiences, leading to inefficient knowledge reuse.
Current agentic systems rely on transient in-context learning or manual engineering, causing agents to frequently "reinvent the wheel" for similar tasks.
There is an absence of a principled framework for validating and maintaining AI skills at scale, making existing repositories prone to unreliable or unsafe content.
results: SkillNet improved the average task reward by 40% and reduced execution steps by 30% compared to the ReAct baseline across ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld environments.
Performance gains were robust across various LLM backbone models, including DeepSeek V3.2, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and o4 Mini, demonstrating complementary capabilities beyond parametric knowledge.

The project releases a large-scale open-source ecosystem, including over 200,000 skills, an interactive platform, and a Python toolkit, to foster community contribution and accelerate AI agent development.
solution: SkillNet introduces an open infrastructure for creating, evaluating, and organizing AI skills through a comprehensive, three-layered Skill Ontology (taxonomy, relation graph, package library).
An automated pipeline abstracts skills from heterogeneous sources like execution trajectories, GitHub repositories, and documents, driven by Large Language Models.

A multi-dimensional evaluation framework rigorously assesses skill quality based on Safety, Completeness, Executability, Maintainability, and Cost-awareness, with automated LLM-based evaluation showing high consistency with human judgment.
paper_summary.summary: SkillNet, an open infrastructure from Zhejiang University and collaborators, provides a unified framework for creating, evaluating, and organizing AI skills. It transitions AI agents from relying on episodic experiences to achieving durable, transferable mastery, leading to an average 40% increase in task reward and a 30% reduction in execution steps across various environments and LLM backbones.

title: SkillNet: Create, Evaluate, and Connect AI Skills

abstract: Real-world tool-using agents operate over long-horizon workflows with recurring structure and diverse demands, where effective behavior requires not only invoking atomic tools but also abstracting, and reusing higher-level tool compositions. However, existing benchmarks mainly measure instance-level success under static tool sets, offering limited insight into agents' ability to acquire such reusable skills. We address this gap by introducing SkillCraft, a benchmark explicitly stress-test agent ability to form and reuse higher-level tool compositions, where we call Skills. SkillCraft features realistic, highly compositional tool-use scenarios with difficulty scaled along both quantitative and structural dimensions, designed to elicit skill abstraction and cross-task reuse. We further propose a lightweight evaluation protocol that enables agents to auto-compose atomic tools into executable Skills, cache and reuse them inside and across tasks, thereby improving efficiency while accumulating a persistent library of reusable skills. Evaluating state-of-the-art agents on SkillCraft, we observe substantial efficiency gains, with token usage reduced by up to 80% by skill saving and reuse. Moreover, success rate strongly correlates with tool composition ability at test time, underscoring compositional skill acquisition as a core capability.

keyInsights: Compositional skill acquisition substantially boosts efficiency, yielding up to 80% token reduction and 75% cost savings for high-performing LLM agents while generally preserving or enhancing task success.
Skills learned demonstrate strong generalizability both across tasks of varying difficulty and between different LLM models, indicating the potential for robust, reusable procedural knowledge.

While beneficial, deeper hierarchical compositions (skills invoking other skills) proved less reliable and efficient than shallow skill libraries due to error propagation and increased debugging complexity.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent benchmarks primarily focus on instance-level task success with static toolsets, overlooking agents' ability to abstract and reuse higher-level tool sequences.
Real-world agent workflows feature long-horizon tasks with recurring substructures, demanding compositional 'Skills' rather than repeated atomic tool calls for efficiency.
A lack of standardized methods to evaluate and encourage LLM agents' acquisition and generalization of complex, reusable tool compositions across diverse tasks.
results: Top-performing models like GPT-5.2 and Claude 4.5 Sonnet achieved average token reductions of 79% and 71% respectively in Skill Mode, with GPT-5.2's success rate improving from 87% to 90%.

Skills transferred across difficulty levels (e.g., from easy to hard tasks) within the same family exhibited high execution success rates (typically 97-100%), validating their reusable procedural structure.

High-quality skills created by capable models (e.g., Claude 4.5 Sonnet) showed 100% success when executed by other diverse models, highlighting that skill creator quality significantly impacts transferability and efficiency gains.
solution: The SkillCraft benchmark was developed with 126 tasks across six difficulty levels, specifically designed to contain recurring substructures that promote the discovery of compositional tool skills.
A "Skill Mode" evaluation protocol provides agents with four minimal primitives (`save skill`, `execute skill`, `list skills`, `get skill`) to manage, store, and invoke learned tool compositions.
A three-stage coding verifier ensures the syntactic correctness, runtime integrity, and quality of newly created skills before they are added to the agent's reusable skill library.
paper_summary.summary: SkillCraft introduces a benchmark and evaluation protocol designed to measure large language model agents' capacity for acquiring and reusing compositional tool skills. The framework demonstrates that stronger LLM agents can achieve up to 80% token reduction and significant cost savings while maintaining or improving task success rates by employing abstractable and reusable skills.

title: SkillCraft: Can LLM Agents Learn to Use Tools Skillfully?

abstract: Building LLM-based agents has become increasingly important. Recent works on LLM-based agent self-evolution primarily record successful experiences as textual prompts or reflections, which cannot reliably guarantee efficient task re-execution in complex scenarios. We propose AgentFactory, a new self-evolution paradigm that preserves successful task solutions as executable subagent code rather than textual experience. Crucially, these subagents are continuously refined based on execution feedback, becoming increasingly robust and efficient as more tasks are encountered. Saved subagents are pure Python code with standardized documentation, enabling portability across any Python-capable system. We demonstrate that AgentFactory enables continuous capability accumulation: its library of executable subagents grows and improves over time, progressively reducing the effort required for similar tasks without manual intervention. Our implementation is open-sourced at this https URL, and our demonstration video is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Preserving successful task solutions as executable code offers a more precise, robust, and reliable mechanism for task re-execution compared to relying on textual descriptions of experience.
Autonomous code modification driven by execution feedback allows subagents to incrementally become more general-purpose and resilient to variations and edge cases.

The design choice to store subagents as pure Python code with standardized documentation (`SKILL.md`) facilitates portability and interoperability, enabling their deployment across diverse AI frameworks.
originalProblem: Existing self-evolving LLM agents primarily record successful experiences as textual prompts or reflections, which are often inefficient and unreliable for guaranteeing robust task re-execution.
Current LLM agent frameworks generally treat agent behavior as static, lacking mechanisms to systematically preserve and utilize knowledge gained from task execution for continuous improvement.
There is a need for robust, reusable, and adaptable solutions for routine procedural tasks that can be autonomously refined over time and are portable across different AI systems.
results: A subagent for README generation demonstrated self-evolution by autonomously replacing a fragile path parsing method with a robust regex-based extraction, improving error handling.

Saved subagents like "Audio Transcriber" and "Document Creator" were successfully migrated to an external system (Claude Code), which learned to use them by interpreting their code and documentation for new complex tasks.

Quantitative evaluation showed AgentFactory significantly reduced orchestration token consumption for new tasks using saved subagents, achieving a 34% reduction for Claude Opus 4.6 (4324 to 2971 tokens) and a 58% reduction for Claude Sonnet 4.6 (9199 to 3862 tokens) compared to initial runs.
solution: AgentFactory implements a three-phase lifecycle (Install, Self-Evolve, Deploy) orchestrated by a Meta-Agent that interacts with a Skill System and a Workspace Manager.
Subagents are constructed, refined, and stored as pure, executable Python code, encapsulating successful task-solving patterns and allowing for autonomous modification based on execution feedback.
The framework employs a unified skill interface comprising Meta Skills (orchestration), Tool Skills (static external tools), and Subagent Skills (dynamically created and refined modules).
paper_summary.summary: AgentFactory is a self-evolving framework enabling large language model (LLM) agents to continuously accumulate and improve task-solving capabilities by creating, refining, and reusing executable subagent code. The framework demonstrated reduced orchestration token consumption by up to 58% when reusing saved subagents for new tasks compared to solving from scratch.

title: AgentFactory: A Self-Evolving Framework Through Executable Subagent Accumulation and Reuse

abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents currently depend on predefined tools or early-stage tool generation, limiting their adaptability and scalability to complex scientific tasks. We introduce CASCADE, a self-evolving agentic framework representing an early instantiation of the transition from "LLM + tool use" to "LLM + skill acquisition". CASCADE enables agents to master complex external tools and codify knowledge through two meta-skills: continuous learning via web search, code extraction, and memory utilization; self-reflection via introspection, knowledge graph exploration, and others. We evaluate CASCADE on SciSkillBench, a benchmark of 116 materials science and chemistry research tasks. CASCADE achieves a 93.3% success rate using GPT-5, compared to 35.4% without evolution mechanisms. We further demonstrate real-world applications in computational analysis, autonomous laboratory experiments, and selective reproduction of published papers. Along with human-agent collaboration and memory consolidation, CASCADE accumulates executable skills that can be shared across agents and scientists, moving toward scalable AI-assisted scientific research.

keyInsights: The transition from "LLM + tool use" to "LLM + skill acquisition" significantly enhances an agent's capability to solve complex scientific problems autonomously.

The synergistic integration of continuous learning (acquiring external knowledge) and comprehensive self-reflection (evaluating solutions, debugging, and adapting strategies) is critical for robust skill acquisition.
A domain-agnostic design with a multi-agent system architecture facilitates broad transferability of learned meta-skills across diverse scientific and engineering disciplines.
originalProblem: Current LLM agents are constrained by static, human-curated tool catalogs and predefined action spaces, limiting their adaptability to novel and complex scientific tasks.
The scalability of LLM agents is hindered by the manual effort required from domain experts to continuously update tool catalogs and craft task-specific prompts.

Existing agentic systems lack robust mechanisms for cumulative skill mastery, sustained learning, and effective integration of human-agent collaboration and memory for scientific knowledge acquisition.
results: CASCADE's DeepSolver subsystem achieved a 93.3% success rate on the SciSkillBench, a 70% improvement compared to the Native LLM baseline's 35.4% success rate using the same GPT-5 model.

The framework consistently outperformed baseline agentic systems, showing an average 17.53% higher overall success rate than the Search&Debug baseline across various models and exhibiting higher accuracy across all six SciSkillBench task categories.

In real-world applications, CASCADE autonomously determined material properties, formulated and validated scientific hypotheses, and operated an autonomous laboratory, processing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data approximately 60-fold faster with higher accuracy (R² = 0.991) than expert-written code.
solution: CASCADE implements a self-evolving multi-agent system, transitioning to an "LLM + skill acquisition" paradigm through continuous learning and self-reflection meta-skills.

Its DeepSolver architecture employs specialized agents for solution research, code execution, and parallel debugging, utilizing tools for web search, code extraction, introspection, and runtime probing.

A hybrid memory system (vector-based and graph-based) enables agents to consolidate and retrieve learned skills and experiences, supported by a natural language interface for human-agent collaboration.
paper_summary.summary: The CASCADE framework, developed by UC Berkeley, LBNL, and EPFL, enables large language model (LLM) agents to autonomously acquire and refine problem-solving skills, moving beyond simple tool use to cumulative skill mastery. It achieved a 93.3% success rate on the SciSkillBench, a benchmark for materials science and chemistry tasks, and demonstrated autonomous operation in real-world lab environments, including analyzing experimental data 60-fold faster than existing expert code.

title: CASCADE: Cumulative Agentic Skill Creation through Autonomous Development and Evolution

abstract: Learning from experience is critical for building capable large language model (LLM) agents, yet prevailing self-evolving paradigms remain inefficient: agents learn in isolation, repeatedly rediscover similar behaviors from limited experience, resulting in redundant exploration and poor generalization. To address this problem, we propose SkillX, a fully automated framework for constructing a \textbf{plug-and-play skill knowledge base} that can be reused across agents and environments. SkillX operates through a fully automated pipeline built on three synergistic innovations: \textit{(i) Multi-Level Skills Design}, which distills raw trajectories into three-tiered hierarchy of strategic plans, functional skills, and atomic skills; \textit{(ii) Iterative Skills Refinement}, which automatically revises skills based on execution feedback to continuously improve library quality; and \textit{(iii) Exploratory Skills Expansion}, which proactively generates and validates novel skills to expand coverage beyond seed training data. Using a strong backbone agent (GLM-4.6), we automatically build a reusable skill library and evaluate its transferability on challenging long-horizon, user-interactive benchmarks, including AppWorld, BFCL-v3, and $\tau^2$-Bench. Experiments show that SkillKB consistently improves task success and execution efficiency when plugged into weaker base agents, highlighting the importance of structured, hierarchical experience representations for generalizable agent learning. Our code will be publicly available soon at this https URL.

keyInsights: A multi-level hierarchical skill design provides a more advantageous and effective experience representation than direct trajectory retrieval or workflows, boosting agent performance.
An iterative refinement process and experience-guided exploration strategy are crucial for continuously improving skill quality, coverage, and sample efficiency in complex environments.
Distilling knowledge from a strong backbone agent into a structured skill base can effectively transfer capabilities to weaker inference models, expanding their operational boundaries.
originalProblem: LLM agents learn inefficiently from isolated experiences, leading to repeated rediscovery of behaviors and poor generalization of learned knowledge, especially in complex, data-scarce environments.
Existing experience representations lack simultaneous transferability, efficient retrieval, and direct executability, hindering effective reuse across tasks.
Prior skill-based systems often demand extensive context management and progressive disclosure, which limits their robustness and practical application.
results: SkillX consistently boosted the performance of various base LLM agents, with weaker models like Qwen3-32B achieving approximately a 10% gain in task success rates.
The multi-level skill representation proved more effective than other experience representations, leading to superior performance and transferability across different base models.

The framework improved agent execution efficiency by reducing the number of execution steps and input tokens, and its experience-guided expansion generated more novel skills and performance gains compared to random exploration.
solution: SkillX, an automated framework, constructs a plug-and-play skill knowledge base to facilitate the reuse of learned behaviors across different agents and environments.
It employs a multi-level hierarchical skill design (Planning, Functional, Atomic) for concise, composable, and robust experience representation.
The framework includes an iterative process of skill extraction, refinement (merging and filtering), and an experience-guided exploratory expansion for continuous improvement and broader coverage.
paper_summary.summary: SkillX is an automated framework that builds a plug-and-play skill knowledge base for LLM agents using a hierarchical representation of planning, functional, and atomic skills. This framework from Zhejiang University and Ant Digital Technologies improved task success rates by approximately 10% for weaker models and enhanced execution efficiency by reducing execution steps and input tokens across complex benchmarks.

title: SkillX: Automatically Constructing Skill Knowledge Bases for Agents

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated effectiveness in code
generation tasks. To enable LLMs to address more complex coding challenges,
existing research has focused on crafting multi-agent systems with agentic
workflows, where complex coding tasks are decomposed into sub-tasks, assigned
to specialized agents. Despite their effectiveness, current approaches heavily
rely on hand-crafted agentic workflows, with both agent topologies and prompts
manually designed, which limits their ability to automatically adapt to
different types of coding problems. To address these limitations and enable
automated workflow design, we propose \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{E}volving
\textbf{W}orkflow (\textbf{SEW}), a novel self-evolving framework that
automatically generates and optimises multi-agent workflows. Extensive
experiments on three coding benchmark datasets, including the challenging
LiveCodeBench, demonstrate that our SEW can automatically design agentic
workflows and optimise them through self-evolution, bringing up to 33\%
improvement on LiveCodeBench compared to using the backbone LLM only.
Furthermore, by investigating different representation schemes of workflow, we
provide insights into the optimal way to encode workflow information with text.

keyInsights: CoRE (Code Representation and Execution) was found to be the most effective workflow representation scheme for producing executable code, achieving a 72.7% Generation Successful Rate.
Both workflow evolution and agent evolution modules contribute to performance, with their combined application yielding up to 20.3% additional improvement over workflow evolution alone.
Hyper Evolution strategies offer greater robustness and lower variance, while Direct Evolution can achieve higher peak performance with more variability.
originalProblem: Existing multi-agent systems for LLM-based code generation rely heavily on manually crafted workflows and agent prompts, which is labor-intensive and limits adaptability.
Traditional approaches struggle with scalability, as different complex programming tasks require unique workflow structures, making bespoke design impractical.
Single-agent LLMs often fall short on complex programming problems that require multi-step reasoning, task decomposition, and collaborative problem-solving.
results: SEW consistently improved code generation performance across HumanEval, MBPP, and LiveCodeBench datasets using GPT-4o mini and Gemini-1.5-pro-002 backbone models.
On the LiveCodeBench dataset, SEW achieved a pass@1 score of 50.9% with GPT-4o mini, representing a 33.9% improvement over the standalone model's 38.0%.
Workflow evolution alone improved performance from 38.0% to 42.3% on LiveCodeBench, with additional gains from agent evolution.
solution: The SEW framework automatically generates and optimizes multi-agent workflows and individual agent prompts based on task descriptions, eliminating manual design.
It employs a Workflow Evolution Module that uses Direct Evolution operators to reconstruct and optimize workflow structural organization.
An Agent Evolution Module utilizes both Direct Evolution and Hyper Evolution operators to refine and optimize individual agent prompts within the workflows.
paper_summary.summary: The Self-Evolving Agentic Workflows (SEW) framework introduces an automated approach for generating and optimizing multi-agent systems for code generation, using evolutionary algorithms to adapt both workflow structures and agent prompts. This framework achieved a 33.9% performance improvement on the LiveCodeBench dataset with GPT-4o mini, reaching a pass@1 score of 50.9%.

title: SEW: Self-Evolving Agentic Workflows for Automated Code Generation

abstract: While skill-centric approaches leverage foundation models to enhance generalization in compositional tasks, they often rely on fixed skill libraries, limiting adaptability to new tasks without manual intervention. To address this, we propose Uni-Skill, a Unified Skill-centric framework that supports skill-aware planning and facilitates automatic skill evolution. Unlike prior methods that restrict planning to predefined skills, Uni-Skill requests for new skill implementations when existing ones are insufficient, ensuring adaptable planning with self-augmented skill library. To support automatic implementation of diverse skills requested by the planning module, we construct SkillFolder, a VerbNet-inspired repository derived from large-scale unstructured robotic videos. SkillFolder introduces a hierarchical skill taxonomy that captures diverse skill descriptions at multiple levels of abstraction. By populating this taxonomy with large-scale, automatically annotated demonstrations, Uni-Skill shifts the paradigm of skill acquisition from inefficient manual annotation to efficient offline structural retrieval. Retrieved examples provide semantic supervision over behavior patterns and fine-grained references for spatial trajectories, enabling few-shot skill inference without deployment-time demonstrations. Comprehensive experiments in both simulation and real-world settings verify the state-of-the-art performance of Uni-Skill over existing VLM-based skill-centric approaches, highlighting its advanced reasoning capabilities and strong zero-shot generalization across a wide range of novel tasks.

keyInsights: Robotic systems can dynamically identify skill gaps and autonomously generate high-level descriptions for necessary new skills.
Unstructured robotic videos can be transformed into actionable skill knowledge through automated annotation and a VerbNet-inspired hierarchical organization (SkillFolder).
Few-shot skill implementation can be achieved by extracting and leveraging fine-grained spatial trajectories and high-level semantic constraints from retrieved skill-centric demonstrations.
originalProblem: Current skill-centric robotic manipulation approaches rely on fixed skill libraries, leading to failures on tasks requiring unforeseen capabilities.
Acquiring new robotic skills is inefficient and unscalable, typically requiring manual human supervision or waypoint annotations.
Abundant unstructured robotic video data cannot be directly leveraged for expanding skill libraries due due to a lack of annotations and structure.
results: Achieved an average success rate of 0.41 on novel tasks in simulation (RLBench), outperforming MOKA (0.10) by 31.0% in zero-shot success and CaP (0.01).
Demonstrated an average success rate of 0.73 in real-world experiments on a Franka Emika robot arm, surpassing MOKA (0.39) with improvements of 20.0% on long-horizon tasks and 34.0% in unseen skills.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical importance of the skill updating mechanism and the referential guidance (semantic constraints and spatial trajectories) from SkillFolder examples for robust generalization.
solution: Introduces Uni-Skill, a Unified Skill-centric framework that integrates Skill-Aware Planning and Automatic Skill Evolution.
Skill-Aware Planning uses a VLM-based discriminator to detect skill gaps, a generator to synthesize new skill descriptions, and a planner to create executable policy code.

Automatic Skill Evolution employs a VLM pipeline for annotating unstructured videos, organizes skills into a hierarchical SkillFolder, and facilitates few-shot skill implementation using retrieved examples and constrained visual prompting.
paper_summary.summary: The Uni-Skill framework enables robots to automatically acquire and implement new manipulation skills from unstructured video data, moving beyond static skill libraries. This system achieved an average success rate of 0.41 on novel tasks in simulation and 0.73 in real-world settings, demonstrating significant improvements over existing baselines.

title: Uni-Skill: Building Self-Evolving Skill Repository for Generalizable Robotic Manipulation

abstract: LLM-based agents depend on effective tool-use policies to solve complex tasks, yet optimizing these policies remains challenging due to delayed supervision and the difficulty of credit assignment in long-horizon trajectories. Existing optimization approaches tend to be either monolithic, which are prone to entangling behaviors, or single-aspect, which ignore cross-module error propagation. To address these limitations, we propose EvoTool, a self-evolving framework that optimizes a modular tool-use policy via a gradient-free evolutionary paradigm. EvoTool decomposes agent's tool-use policy into four modules, including Planner, Selector, Caller, and Synthesizer, and iteratively improves them in a self-improving loop through three novel mechanisms.
