Trajectory-Grounded Blame Attribution uses diagnostic traces to localize failures to a specific module. Feedback-Guided Targeted Mutation then edits only that module via natural-language critique. Diversity-Aware Population Selection preserves complementary candidates to ensure solution diversity. Across four benchmarks, EvoTool outperforms strong baselines by over 5 points on both GPT-4.1 and Qwen3-8B, while achieving superior efficiency and transferability. The code will be released once paper is accepted.

keyInsights: Decomposing tool-use policies into modular components allows for targeted, stable improvements by addressing specific failures without destabilizing unrelated functionalities.
Language models can effectively diagnose and attribute blame to specific modules within complex interaction trajectories, enabling precise feedback for policy refinement.

Explicit natural-language feedback, grounded in interaction trajectories, is highly effective for localized and interpretable mutation of module specifications, contributing more to performance than raw trajectory evidence alone.

Maintaining diversity in the population of candidate policies through instance-wise winner selection is crucial for robust evolutionary optimization, particularly for heterogeneous task distributions, preventing premature convergence.
originalProblem: Optimizing tool-use policies in LLM agents is challenging due to delayed supervision and the credit assignment problem, making it difficult to pinpoint specific failure sources in long interaction sequences.
Existing monolithic policy optimization methods risk 'entangled behaviors,' where fixing one error inadvertently destabilizes other functionalities, leading to unstable improvements.
Single-aspect optimization methods, which refine individual components in isolation, often overlook cross-module error propagation, limiting their effectiveness in long-horizon tasks.
results: EVOTOOL consistently outperforms existing hand-crafted, monolithic, and single-aspect optimization methods, achieving an average of 70.6 on GPT-4.1, nearly 6 points higher than the best single-aspect baseline and 7 points higher than the best monolithic baseline.
The framework demonstrates efficient learning dynamics, achieving superior performance with significantly lower token costs compared to monolithic optimizers, confirming its cost-effectiveness.

Ablation studies confirm the critical roles of blame attribution, feedback-guided mutation, and diversity-aware population selection in EVOTOOL's robust multi-task tool-policy learning, showing performance degradation when these components are removed.

Learned policies exhibit strong transferability across different datasets (e.g., ToolBench to RestBench) and different backbone LLMs (e.g., Qwen3-8B to GPT-4.1), indicating generalizable tool-use behaviors rather than narrow overfitting.
solution: EVOTOOL proposes a self-evolving, gradient-free evolutionary framework that iteratively optimizes a modularized tool-use policy composed of Planner, Selector, Caller, and Synthesizer modules.
It employs a 'Blamer LLM' for trajectory-grounded blame attribution to precisely identify the module most responsible for a failure within an episode record.

A 'Mutator LLM' generates natural-language feedback for feedback-guided targeted mutation, editing only the blamed module's specification to create a refined child candidate, while diversity-aware population selection preserves heterogeneous policy variants.
paper_summary.summary: EVOTOOL introduces a self-evolving framework for optimizing modular tool-use policies in LLM agents, utilizing blame attribution and targeted mutation to address delayed supervision and credit assignment. The framework achieves superior performance, for instance, a 6-7 point improvement over leading baselines on GPT-4.1, and demonstrates strong transferability across tasks and models.

title: EvoTool: Self-Evolving Tool-Use Policy Optimization in LLM Agents via Blame-Aware Mutation and Diversity-Aware Selection

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) based agents are powerful yet fundamentally static after deployment, lacking the ability to autonomously expand capabilities, generate new tools, or evolve their reasoning. This work introduces a hierarchical self-evolving multi-agent framework that integrates a Base LLM, an operational SLM agent, a Code-Generation LLM, and a Teacher-LLM to enable continuous adaptation. The workflow begins with the agent attempting a task using reasoning and existing tools; if unsuccessful, it escalates to tool synthesis through the Code-Gen LLM, and when failures persist, it triggers an evolution phase using Curriculum Learning (CL), Reward-Based Learning (RL), or Genetic Algorithm (GA) evolution. Using the TaskCraft dataset rich in hierarchical tasks, tool-use traces, and difficulty scaling we evaluate these paradigms. CL delivers fast recovery and strong generalization, RL excels on high-difficulty tasks, and GA offers high behavioral diversity. Across all settings, evolved agents outperform their originals, demonstrating robust, autonomous, self-improving agentic evolution.

keyInsights: Integrating dynamic tool generation and adaptive learning paradigms into a hierarchical multi-agent system provides a pragmatic approach to overcoming the staticity of current LLM agents and advancing towards Artificial General Intelligence.

A multi-faceted approach, leveraging the distinct strengths of Curriculum Learning for efficiency and generalization, Reward-Based Learning for high-difficulty task mastery, and Genetic Algorithms for exploration and diversity, yields comprehensive self-improvement.
Structured feedback loops, verifiable performance metrics, and systematic agent lifecycle management are crucial for enabling scalable, robust, and continuously improving autonomous AI systems.
originalProblem: Existing Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents are static after deployment, operating with fixed architectures, prompts, and workflows.
This static nature severely limits their ability to adapt to novel tasks, dynamic environments, or evolving knowledge distributions.
Current agents lack mechanisms for autonomous capability expansion, reasoning evolution, and continuous improvement, hindering their long-term autonomy and robustness.
results: Evolved agents across all learning paradigms (Curriculum Learning, Reward-Based Learning, Genetic Algorithms) consistently and significantly outperformed their original, static counterparts in task success rates on the TaskCraft dataset.
Curriculum Learning demonstrated the highest overall learning efficiency, fastest convergence, and strongest generalization across varying task difficulties, showing the most efficient tool usage.

Reward-Based Learning achieved superior performance on high-difficulty tasks, and Genetic Algorithms exhibited the greatest behavioral diversity and occasional large, rapid performance jumps across generations.
solution: A hierarchical multi-agent framework orchestrates a Base LLM, Operational Agent, Code-Generation LLM, and Teacher-LLM for continuous adaptation and improvement.
The system implements an escalation model: agents first attempt a task, then a Code-Generation LLM synthesizes new tools if needed, and finally, an evolution phase is triggered if failures persist.

The evolution phase utilizes Curriculum Learning, Reward-Based Learning, or Genetic Algorithms to train cloned agents, which are then rigorously evaluated and promoted to replace predecessors if performance improves significantly.
paper_summary.summary: Rastrai researchers developed a hierarchical framework for self-evolving multi-agent Large Language Model systems, enabling agents to autonomously adapt, generate new tools, and refine reasoning. Evaluations using the TaskCraft dataset showed that agents evolved through curriculum learning, reward-based learning, or genetic algorithms consistently surpassed static agents in task success rates, tool efficiency, and overall performance.

title: Towards AGI A Pragmatic Approach Towards Self Evolving Agent

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are moving beyond static uses and are now powering agents that learn continually during their interaction with external environments. For example, agents can learn reusable skills while navigating web pages or toggling new tools. However, existing methods for skill learning often create skills that are over-specialized to a single website and fail to generalize. We introduce PolySkill, a new framework that enables agents to learn generalizable and compositional skills. The core idea, inspired by polymorphism in software engineering, is to decouple a skill's abstract goal (what it accomplishes) and its concrete implementation (how it is executed). Experiments show that our method (1) improves skill reuse by 1.7x on seen websites and (2) boosts success rates by up to 9.4% on Mind2Web and 13.9% on unseen websites, while reducing steps by over 20%. (3) In self-exploration settings without specified tasks, our framework improves the quality of proposed tasks and enables agents to learn generalizable skills that work across different sites. By enabling the agent to identify and refine its own goals, the PolySkill enhances the agent's ability to learn a better curriculum, leading to the acquisition of more generalizable skills compared to baseline methods. This work provides a practical path toward building agents capable of continual learning in adaptive environments. Our findings show that separating a skill's goal from its execution is a crucial step toward developing autonomous agents that can learn and generalize across the open web continuously. Our code can be found in this https URL.

keyInsights: Polymorphic abstraction enables the creation of generalizable skills that are reusable across diverse web environments, moving beyond over-specialized approaches.
Structuring skill learning through a hierarchical, abstract-to-concrete approach accelerates skill acquisition on unseen websites by providing focused exploration goals.
Effective skill induction, which emphasizes reusability and compositionality, directly correlates with improved task efficiency (fewer steps) and overall task success rates.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based web agents acquire skills that are often over-specialized, failing to generalize across diverse websites or even varied tasks within the same functional domain.
Current skill learning methods produce skills tied to specific UI elements, hindering adaptation to new web environments and limiting effective continual learning.
Evaluation metrics primarily focus on final task success, failing to adequately diagnose skill transfer failures or quantify the reusability and compositional value of learned skills.
results: PolySkill consistently outperformed baselines like ASI across Mind2Web and WebArena benchmarks, achieving up to a 9.4% higher success rate on Mind2Web and a 4.9% overall performance advantage over ASI on WebArena in continual learning scenarios.

The framework demonstrated significantly improved skill generalizability, with 73% of learned skills transferring to unseen websites, a substantial increase from less than 31% for prior methods, and reduced operational steps by over 20%.

PolySkill effectively mitigated catastrophic forgetting in continual learning settings and enabled autonomous skill acquisition in task-free explorative environments, achieving 43.1% success on held-out shopping benchmarks and 66.2% on GitLab, and 84% on GitHub.
solution: Introduces the PolySkill framework, which leverages polymorphic abstraction from software engineering to decouple a skill's abstract goal from its site-specific implementation.

Establishes a domain-driven skill hierarchy with abstract classes defining high-level goals and method signatures, and concrete subclasses providing distinct, website-specific implementations for these methods.

Guides the LLM-based agent to first induce abstract class interfaces for a new domain and then define concrete skill implementations within site-specific classes that inherit from these abstract blueprints.
paper_summary.summary: PolySkill is a framework that enhances the generalizability of skills for LLM-based web agents by decoupling abstract goals from concrete, site-specific implementations. The framework achieves up to a 9.4% increase in task success rate on Mind2Web benchmarks and improves skill transfer to unseen websites by 73% compared to previous methods, while reducing operational steps by over 20%.

title: PolySkill: Learning Generalizable Skills Through Polymorphic Abstraction

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant progress in handling complex programming tasks. However, current methods rely on manual model selection and fixed workflows, which limit their ability to adapt to changing task complexities. To address this, we propose SEMAG, a Self-Evolutionary Multi-Agent code Generation framework that mimics human coding practices. It decomposes programming tasks into stages, including planning, coding, debugging, and discussion, while adapting workflows to task difficulty. Its self-evolutionary agents can access the latest models in real time and automatically upgrade the backbone model. SEMAG sets new state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy across benchmarks. Using identical backbone models, SEMAG outperforms prior methods by 3.3% on CodeContests. When augmented with self-evolutionary model selection that automatically identifies optimal backbones, SEMAG reaches 52.6%, showcasing both framework effectiveness and adaptability to evolving LLM capabilities.

keyInsights: Dynamically adjusting the reasoning depth and employing a multi-agent collaborative refinement process, including discussion and decision modules, significantly improves code generation performance and allows escaping local minima.
Automated self-evolution agents can effectively identify and deploy optimal, evolving backbone LLMs in real-time, enabling continuous adaptation and performance enhancement.
Trace-guided debugging combined with semantic analysis and targeted suggestions is crucial for effective iterative code refinement, leading to more robust solutions.
originalProblem: Existing multi-agent code generation frameworks often use fixed reasoning depths, leading to inefficiency for simple tasks and insufficient reasoning for complex ones.
Many debugging pipelines employ only a single iteration, which can lead to local minima when initial code output is significantly flawed.
Current LLM-based code generation systems are tightly coupled to a single static backbone model, limiting their adaptability to evolving LLM capabilities or changing task characteristics.
results: SEMAG achieved new state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy on all seven benchmarks, including 98.8% on HumanEval (GPT-4o) and improvements of 1.8-6.0% over previous best baselines (LPW) on foundational benchmarks.
The self-evolution mechanism successfully identified Claude-3.7-Sonnet as a superior backbone for the CodeContests benchmark, achieving 52.6% Pass@1 accuracy compared to 38.0% with GPT-4o.

The hierarchical prompting strategy reduced token consumption by 19.3% on HumanEval and 15.5% on MBPP compared to LPW, demonstrating improved resource efficiency for simpler tasks while maintaining or improving accuracy.
solution: SEMAG implements a four-level hierarchical code synthesis framework that progressively increases computational effort based on task complexity, starting from direct generation to multi-agent collaborative refinement.
An adaptive level transition mechanism, based on execution trace similarity, dynamically adjusts the reasoning depth, ensuring flexible resource allocation rather than fixed iteration thresholds.

A self-evolution mechanism uses parallel selector agents to autonomously identify and select the most suitable, up-to-date backbone LLMs for specific tasks by evaluating candidates based on sampled performance and reaching consensus.
paper_summary.summary: A hierarchical multi-agent framework named SEMAG is introduced for code generation, dynamically adapting reasoning depth and selecting optimal large language models. It achieved state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy across seven benchmarks, with improvements up to 6.8% over previous baselines on MBPP-ET (GPT-3.5) and 5.7% on competition-level tasks (GPT-4o), demonstrating enhanced resource efficiency and adaptability.

title: SEMAG: Self-Evolutionary Multi-Agent Code Generation

abstract: Training large language model (LLM) agents to acquire necessary skills and perform diverse tasks within an environment is gaining interest as a means to enable open-endedness. However, creating the training dataset for their skill acquisition faces several challenges. Manual trajectory collection requires significant human effort. Another approach, where LLMs directly propose tasks to learn, is often invalid, as the LLMs lack knowledge of which tasks are actually feasible. Moreover, the generated data may not provide a meaningful learning signal, as agents often already perform well on the proposed tasks. To address this, we propose a novel automatic skill discovery framework EXIF for LLM-powered agents, designed to improve the feasibility of generated target behaviors while accounting for the agents' capabilities. Our method adopts an exploration-first strategy by employing an exploration agent (Alice) to train the target agent (Bob) to learn essential skills in the environment. Specifically, Alice first interacts with the environment to retrospectively generate a feasible, environment-grounded skill dataset, which is then used to train Bob. Crucially, we incorporate an iterative feedback loop, where Alice evaluates Bob's performance to identify areas for improvement. This feedback then guides Alice's next round of exploration, forming a closed-loop data generation process. Experiments on Webshop and Crafter demonstrate EXIF's ability to effectively discover meaningful skills and iteratively expand the capabilities of the trained agent without any human intervention, achieving substantial performance improvements. Interestingly, we observe that setting Alice to the same model as Bob also notably improves performance, demonstrating EXIF's potential for building a self-evolving system.

keyInsights: An "exploration-first" approach is crucial for generating feasible and environmentally grounded tasks, significantly outperforming "proposal-first" methods in terms of task validity.
Iterative feedback is essential for continuous performance improvement, guiding the exploration agent to generate data that specifically addresses the learning agent's evolving needs and weaknesses.

The framework facilitates the emergence of self-evolving agent systems, demonstrating substantial performance gains even when the same, smaller LLM acts as both the explorer (Alice) and the learner (Bob).
originalProblem: Manual creation of instruction-trajectory datasets for training LLM agents is costly and does not scale.
Existing LLM-based synthetic data generation methods often propose infeasible or irrelevant tasks due to a lack of environmental grounding.
LLM agents frequently struggle with continuous, autonomous skill acquisition and expansion in open-ended environments without significant human intervention.
results: EXIF significantly improved the performance of smaller LLMs (e.g., Llama3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B) in Webshop and Crafter, with Llama3.1-8B's reward increasing from 2.0 to 53.7 in Webshop.
Exploration-first strategies achieved high task feasibility (e.g., 88.0% in Webshop, 93.0% in Crafter) compared to "proposal-first" methods (22.0% in Webshop, 45.0% in Crafter).
The system showed consistent iterative performance gains and refined skill acquisition, with feedback guiding Alice towards generating increasingly complex and targeted skills over iterations.
solution: Introduces EXploration and Iterative Feedback (EXIF), an iterative framework employing two LLM agents: Alice (exploration and instruction generation) and Bob (task performing).
Alice uses an "exploration-first" strategy to interact with the environment, generating feasible action trajectories from which natural language instructions are then retrospectively created.
An iterative feedback loop allows Alice to analyze Bob's performance, generate targeted natural language feedback on deficiencies, and guide subsequent exploration for more relevant data generation.
paper_summary.summary: A framework for automated skill discovery in language agents, EXIF, utilizes an exploration-first strategy and iterative feedback to enable continuous learning and expansion of capabilities in complex environments. It demonstrably improves the performance of target LLMs in environments like Webshop and Crafter, even when using the same smaller model for both exploration and learning.

title: Automated Skill Discovery for Language Agents through Exploration and Iterative Feedback

abstract: Current LLM coding agents are predominantly trained on composite benchmarks (e.g., bug fixing), which often leads to task-specific overfitting and limited generalization. To address this, we propose a novel scaling paradigm that shifts the focus from task-level optimization to atomic skill mastery. We first formalize five fundamental atomic skills, code localization, code editing, unit-test generation, issue reproduction, and code review, that serve as the basis vectors for complex software engineering tasks. Compared with composite coding tasks, these atomic skills are more generalizable and composable. Then, we scale coding agents by performing joint RL over atomic skills. In this manner, atomic skills are consistently improved without negative interference or trade-offs between them. Notably, we observe that improvements in these atomic skills generalize well to other unseen composite coding tasks, such as bug-fixing, code refactoring, machine learning engineering, and code security. The observation motivates a new scaling paradigm for coding agents by training with atomic skills. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed paradigm. Notably, our joint RL improves average performance by 18.7% on 5 atomic skills and 5 composite tasks.

keyInsights: Complex software engineering tasks can be effectively broken down into a finite set of fundamental, generalizable, and independently evaluable atomic skills.

Joint reinforcement learning, even across heterogeneous atomic skills, can lead to consistent performance improvements on all skills without negative interference, demonstrating positive transfer of learning.
Mastery of these foundational atomic skills through joint training enables compositional generalization, allowing agents to perform effectively on unseen, complex composite tasks.
originalProblem: Current LLM coding agents, typically trained on composite benchmarks, often exhibit task-specific overfitting, leading to limited generalization across different software engineering tasks (e.g., bug-fixing capabilities not transferring to code refactoring).

The "black-box" nature of composite task training lacks explicit supervision on intermediate steps, potentially leading agents to learn brittle policies based on memorization rather than robust problem-solving.

Scaling reinforcement learning on the vast diversity of real-world composite software engineering tasks is challenging due to the impracticality of defining dense reward functions for every new domain.
results: The joint RL framework improved average performance across the five atomic skills by 18.7% compared to the Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) initialized model.

Significant compositional generalization was observed, with consistent performance gains across five out-of-distribution (OOD) composite tasks, including SWE-bench (bug-fixing), Terminal-Bench, Code Refactoring, and SEC-bench (security tasks).

An ablation study demonstrated that joint atomic-skills RL achieved broader and more widespread improvements across atomic skills and OOD benchmarks compared to single-task RL, which tended towards specialization without broad generalization.
solution: Complex software engineering tasks are decomposed into five fundamental, composable "atomic skills": Code Localization, Code Editing, Unit-Test Generation, Issue Reproduction, and Code Review, each with a precisely defined input, output, and reward function.

A joint reinforcement learning (RL) framework trains a single policy across all atomic skills simultaneously, optimizing a shared objective that maximizes aggregated rewards without requiring skill-specific heads or separate optimizers.

The training leverages Group-based Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for stability and sample efficiency, supported by an optimized infrastructure featuring a sandboxed execution environment, minimal `bash` and `str replace` tool scaffolding, and decoupled rollout and training workers.
paper_summary.summary: A research collaboration introduces a scaling paradigm for LLM coding agents that focuses on mastering five fundamental "atomic skills" through joint reinforcement learning. This approach improved average performance on atomic skills by 18.7% and showed compositional generalization to diverse, unseen composite coding tasks.

title: Scaling Coding Agents via Atomic Skills

To address this, we propose SEMAG, a Self-Evolutionary Multi-Agent code Generation framework that mimics human coding practices. title: SEMAG: Self-Evolutionary Multi-Agent Code Generation

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) Agents, often trained with Reinforcement Learning (RL), are constrained by a dependency on human-curated data, limiting scalability and tethering AI to human knowledge. Existing self-evolution frameworks offer an alternative but are typically restricted by the model's inherent capabilities and single-round interactions, hindering the development of complex curricula involving tool use or dynamic reasoning. We introduce Agent0, a fully autonomous framework that evolves high-performing agents without external data through multi-step co-evolution and seamless tool integration. Agent0 establishes a symbiotic competition between two agents initialized from the same base LLM: a curriculum agent that proposes increasingly challenging frontier tasks, and an executor agent that learns to solve them. We integrate external tools to enhance the executor's problem-solving capacity; this improvement, in turn, pressures the curriculum agent to construct more complex, tool-aware tasks. Through this iterative process, Agent0 establishes a self-reinforcing cycle that continuously produces high-quality curricula. Empirically, Agent0 substantially boosts reasoning capabilities, improving the Qwen3-8B-Base model by 18% on mathematical reasoning and 24% on general reasoning benchmarks. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: A symbiotic co-evolutionary loop between a task-generating and a task-solving agent, explicitly incentivizing tool use, can drive continuous learning and overcome inherent capability ceilings without external data.
Ambiguity-aware policy optimization, which scales training signals and adjusts trust regions based on self-consistency, is crucial for robust learning from noisy, self-generated pseudo-labels.

Seamless, multi-turn integration of external tools enables agents to perform iterative reasoning, self-correction, and hybrid problem-solving by combining natural language with programmatic execution.
originalProblem: LLM agent training relies on vast, human-curated datasets, creating scalability bottlenecks and limiting AI potential to human knowledge.
Existing self-evolution frameworks suffer from capability ceilings as models generate tasks that do not sufficiently challenge their current abilities, leading to learning stagnation.
These frameworks often lack the dynamic, multi-step interaction and complex tool use capabilities necessary for real-world problem-solving.
results: Agent0 improves the Qwen3-8B-Base model's performance by 18% on mathematical reasoning benchmarks and 24% on general reasoning benchmarks.
It consistently outperforms various state-of-the-art self-evolving baselines and base models (with and without zero-shot tool access) across diverse benchmarks.
The framework exhibits stable and progressive performance improvement across co-evolutionary iterations, with generated tasks showing increased difficulty and a higher frequency of tool utilization.
solution: Agent0 implements a fully autonomous, co-evolutionary framework featuring a Curriculum Agent that generates challenging tasks and an Executor Agent that solves them.
It integrates external tools into a multi-turn reasoning process, allowing the Executor to execute code in a sandboxed environment and receive iterative feedback.
The Executor Agent is trained using Ambiguity-Dynamic Policy Optimization (ADPO), a modified GRPO approach designed to handle the inherent noise and ambiguity of self-generated pseudo-labels.
paper_summary.summary: The Agent0 framework establishes a fully autonomous method for evolving Large Language Model agents from zero external data, using a co-evolutionary competition between a curriculum agent and an executor agent with integrated tool use. It achieves substantial performance improvements on mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating enhanced generalization capabilities.

title: Agent0: Unleashing Self-Evolving Agents from Zero Data via Tool-Integrated Reasoning

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in organizing and executing complex tasks, and many such agents are now widely used in various application scenarios. However, developing these agents requires carefully designed workflows, carefully crafted prompts, and iterative tuning, which requires LLM techniques and domain-specific expertise. These hand-crafted limitations hinder the scalability and cost-effectiveness of LLM agents across a wide range of industries. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{InfiAgent}, a Pyramid-like DAG-based Multi-Agent Framework that can be applied to \textbf{infi}nite scenarios, which introduces several key innovations: a generalized "agent-as-a-tool" mechanism that automatically decomposes complex agents into hierarchical multi-agent systems; a dual-audit mechanism that ensures the quality and stability of task completion; an agent routing function that enables efficient task-agent matching; and an agent self-evolution mechanism that autonomously restructures the agent DAG based on new tasks, poor performance, or optimization opportunities. Furthermore, InfiAgent's atomic task design supports agent parallelism, significantly improving execution efficiency. This framework evolves into a versatile pyramid-like multi-agent system capable of solving a wide range of problems. Evaluations on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that InfiAgent achieves 9.9\% higher performance compared to ADAS (similar auto-generated agent framework), while a case study of the AI research assistant InfiHelper shows that it generates scientific papers that have received recognition from human reviewers at top-tier IEEE conferences.
title: InfiAgent: Self-Evolving Pyramid Agent Framework for Infinite Scenarios

abstract: Autonomous agents powered by large language models (LLMs) have the potential to significantly enhance human productivity by reasoning, using tools, and executing complex tasks in diverse environments. However, current approaches to developing such agents remain costly and inefficient, as they typically require manually constructed task datasets and reinforcement learning (RL) pipelines with extensive random exploration. These limitations lead to prohibitively high data-construction costs, low exploration efficiency, and poor sample utilization. To address these challenges, we present AgentEvolver, a self-evolving agent system that leverages the semantic understanding and reasoning capabilities of LLMs to drive autonomous agent learning. AgentEvolver introduces three synergistic mechanisms: (i) self-questioning, which enables curiosity-driven task generation in novel environments, reducing dependence on handcrafted datasets; (ii) self-navigating, which improves exploration efficiency through experience reuse and hybrid policy guidance; and (iii) self-attributing, which enhances sample efficiency by assigning differentiated rewards to trajectory states and actions based on their contribution. By integrating these mechanisms into a unified framework, AgentEvolver enables scalable, cost-effective, and continual improvement of agent capabilities. Preliminary experiments indicate that AgentEvolver achieves more efficient exploration, better sample utilization, and faster adaptation compared to traditional RL-based baselines.
title: AgentEvolver: Towards Efficient Self-Evolving Agent System

title: Agent0: Unleashing Self-Evolving Agents from Zero Data via Tool-Integrated Reasoning

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown stunning results in complex tasks, yet they often operate in isolation, failing to learn from past experiences. Existing memory-based methods primarily store raw trajectories, which are often redundant and noise-heavy. This prevents agents from extracting high-level, reusable behavioral patterns that are essential for generalization. In this paper, we propose SkillRL, a framework that bridges the gap between raw experience and policy improvement through automatic skill discovery and recursive evolution. Our approach introduces an experience-based distillation mechanism to build a hierarchical skill library SkillBank, an adaptive retrieval strategy for general and task-specific heuristics, and a recursive evolution mechanism that allows the skill library to co-evolve with the agent's policy during reinforcement learning. These innovations significantly reduce the token footprint while enhancing reasoning utility. Experimental results on ALFWorld, WebShop and seven search-augmented tasks demonstrate that SkillRL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines over 15.3% and maintaining robustness as task complexity increases. Code is available at this this https URL.
title: SkillRL: Evolving Agents via Recursive Skill-Augmented Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown stunning results in complex tasks, yet they often operate in isolation, failing to learn from past experiences. Existing memory-based methods primarily store raw trajectories, which are often redundant and noise-heavy. This prevents agents from extracting high-level, reusable behavioral patterns that are essential for generalization. In this paper, we propose SkillRL, a framework that bridges the gap between raw experience and policy improvement through automatic skill discovery and recursive evolution. Our approach introduces an experience-based distillation mechanism to build a hierarchical skill library SkillBank, an adaptive retrieval strategy for general and task-specific heuristics, and a recursive evolution mechanism that allows the skill library to co-evolve with the agent's policy during reinforcement learning. These innovations significantly reduce the token footprint while enhancing reasoning utility. Experimental results on ALFWorld, WebShop and seven search-augmented tasks demonstrate that SkillRL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines over 15.3% and maintaining robustness as task complexity increases. Code is available at this this https URL.
title: SkillRL: Evolving Agents via Recursive Skill-Augmented Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Most Large Language Model (LLM) agent memory systems rely on a small set of static, hand-designed operations for extracting memory. These fixed procedures hard-code human priors about what to store and how to revise memory, making them rigid under diverse interaction patterns and inefficient on long histories. To this end, we present \textbf{MemSkill}, which reframes these operations as learnable and evolvable memory skills, structured and reusable routines for extracting, consolidating, and pruning information from interaction traces. Inspired by the design philosophy of agent skills, MemSkill employs a \emph{controller} that learns to select a small set of relevant skills, paired with an LLM-based \emph{executor} that produces skill-guided memories. Beyond learning skill selection, MemSkill introduces a \emph{designer} that periodically reviews hard cases where selected skills yield incorrect or incomplete memories, and evolves the skill set by proposing refinements and new skills. Together, MemSkill forms a closed-loop procedure that improves both the skill-selection policy and the skill set itself. Experiments on LoCoMo, LongMemEval, HotpotQA, and ALFWorld demonstrate that MemSkill improves task performance over strong baselines and generalizes well across settings. Further analyses shed light on how skills evolve, offering insights toward more adaptive, self-evolving memory management for LLM agents.
title: MemSkill: Learning and Evolving Memory Skills for Self-Evolving Agents

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant progress in handling complex programming tasks. However, current methods rely on manual model selection and fixed workflows, which limit their ability to adapt to changing task complexities. To address this, we propose SEMAG, a Self-Evolutionary Multi-Agent code Generation framework that mimics human coding practices. It decomposes programming tasks into stages, including planning, coding, debugging, and discussion, while adapting workflows to task difficulty. Its self-evolutionary agents can access the latest models in real time and automatically upgrade the backbone model. SEMAG sets new state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy across benchmarks. Using identical backbone models, SEMAG outperforms prior methods by 3.3% on CodeContests. When augmented with self-evolutionary model selection that automatically identifies optimal backbones, SEMAG reaches 52.6%, showcasing both framework effectiveness and adaptability to evolving LLM capabilities.
title: SEMAG: Self-Evolutionary Multi-Agent Code Generation

abstract: Let $F_*$ be a homology theory of finite type and $\{E^r_{s,t}(X)=\frac{Z^r_{s,t}(X)}{B^r_{s,t}(X)}; d^r_{s,t}\}$ denote the Atiyah-Hirzebruch spectral sequence for $F_*$ of a $CW$-complex $X$. In this paper we study elements of the maximal possible order in the groups $E^2_{n,0}(X)/Z^{r}_{n,0}(X)$.

title: Highly divisible cycles in homological Atiyah-Hirzebruch spectral sequences

abstract: Contemporary evaluation techniques are inadequate for agentic systems. These approaches either focus exclusively on final outcomes -- ignoring the step-by-step nature of agentic systems, or require excessive manual labour. To address this, we introduce the Agent-as-a-Judge framework, wherein agentic systems are used to evaluate agentic systems. This is an organic extension of the LLM-as-a-Judge framework, incorporating agentic features that enable intermediate feedback for the entire task-solving process. We apply the Agent-as-a-Judge to the task of code generation. To overcome issues with existing benchmarks and provide a proof-of-concept testbed for Agent-as-a-Judge, we present DevAI, a new benchmark of 55 realistic automated AI development tasks. It includes rich manual annotations, like a total of 365 hierarchical user requirements. We benchmark three of the popular agentic systems using Agent-as-a-Judge and find it dramatically outperforms LLM-as-a-Judge and is as reliable as our human evaluation baseline. Altogether, we believe that Agent-as-a-Judge marks a concrete step forward for modern agentic systems -- by providing rich and reliable reward signals necessary for dynamic and scalable self-improvement.

keyInsights: Agent-as-a-Judge provides more reliable and human-aligned evaluations compared to traditional LLM-as-a-Judge, demonstrating its suitability for assessing complex, multi-step agentic tasks.

The framework's ability to offer rich, intermediate feedback throughout an agent's task-solving process is crucial for effective reinforcement learning, process-supervised reward models, and dynamic debugging.

The DevAI benchmark effectively challenges current state-of-the-art AI developer agents, emphasizing the need for evaluation environments that move beyond simple code generation to full development cycles.
originalProblem: Existing evaluation methods for AI, including LLM-as-a-Judge frameworks, are primarily outcome-focused and fail to provide rich, intermediate feedback for complex, multi-step agentic systems.
Human evaluation for agentic systems is prohibitively expensive, time-consuming, and prone to individual biases, making it difficult to scale for rapid AI development.

Current benchmarks for code generation or software engineering tasks do not adequately reflect the full complexity of real-world AI application development, often focusing on isolated problems rather than complete development cycles.
results: Agent-as-a-Judge achieved an alignment rate of up to 92.07% with human consensus, significantly outperforming LLM-as-a-Judge (up to 70.76%) and even individual human evaluators.
The framework demonstrated substantial efficiency gains, reducing evaluation time by 97.72% and cost by 97.64% compared to expert human evaluators.

State-of-the-art open-source developer agents (GPT-Pilot, OpenHands) achieved only approximately 29% satisfaction of DevAI task requirements, confirming the benchmark's challenging nature and the need for further agent development.
solution: The "Agent-as-a-Judge" framework, an extension of LLM-as-a-Judge, employs an agentic system to evaluate other agentic systems, capturing step-by-step behavior and providing process-oriented feedback.

A new benchmark, DevAI (the AI Developer Dataset), was created with 55 comprehensive AI application development tasks, featuring hierarchical requirements and explicit dependencies to enable non-sparse evaluation.

The judge agent's architecture incorporates modular components like Graph, Read, Retrieve, Ask, and Locate to analyze development workspaces, understand project structures, and extract evidence from agent trajectories for verification.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Meta AI and King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST) developed the "Agent-as-a-Judge" framework and the DevAI benchmark to provide a scalable and reliable method for evaluating agentic AI systems. This approach offers fine-grained, step-by-step feedback, achieving over 90% alignment with human consensus while reducing evaluation costs and time by more than 97%.

title: Agent-as-a-Judge: Evaluate Agents with Agents

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are moving beyond static uses and are now powering agents that learn continually during their interaction with external environments. For example, agents can learn reusable skills while navigating web pages or toggling new tools. However, existing methods for skill learning often create skills that are over-specialized to a single website and fail to generalize. We introduce PolySkill, a new framework that enables agents to learn generalizable and compositional skills. The core idea, inspired by polymorphism in software engineering, is to decouple a skill's abstract goal (what it accomplishes) and its concrete implementation (how it is executed). Experiments show that our method (1) improves skill reuse by 1.7x on seen websites and (2) boosts success rates by up to 9.4% on Mind2Web and 13.9% on unseen websites, while reducing steps by over 20%. (3) In self-exploration settings without specified tasks, our framework improves the quality of proposed tasks and enables agents to learn generalizable skills that work across different sites. By enabling the agent to identify and refine its own goals, the PolySkill enhances the agent's ability to learn a better curriculum, leading to the acquisition of more generalizable skills compared to baseline methods. This work provides a practical path toward building agents capable of continual learning in adaptive environments. Our findings show that separating a skill's goal from its execution is a crucial step toward developing autonomous agents that can learn and generalize across the open web continuously.

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) Agents, often trained with Reinforcement Learning (RL), are constrained by a dependency on human-curated data, limiting scalability and tethering AI to human knowledge. Existing self-evolution frameworks offer an alternative but are typically restricted by the model's inherent capabilities and single-round interactions, hindering the development of complex curricula involving tool use or dynamic reasoning. We introduce Agent0, a fully autonomous framework that evolves high-performing agents without external data through multi-step co-evolution and seamless tool integration. Agent0 establishes a symbiotic competition between two agents initialized from the same base LLM: a curriculum agent that proposes increasingly challenging frontier tasks, and an executor agent that learns to solve them. We integrate external tools to enhance the executor's problem-solving capacity; this improvement, in turn, pressures the curriculum agent to construct more complex, tool-aware tasks. Through this iterative process, Agent0 establishes a self-reinforcing cycle that continuously produces high-quality curricula. Empirically, Agent0 substantially boosts reasoning capabilities, improving the Qwen3-8B-Base model by 18% on mathematical reasoning and 24% on general reasoning benchmarks. Code is available at this https URL.
title: Agent0: Unleashing Self-Evolving Agents from Zero Data via Tool-Integrated Reasoning
